role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2082 Tax Preparers 4.2% of the 4-digit 5412 wage bill — but 5412 is dominated by CPA firms (13-2011 Accountants 34.2%); within 541213 proper, preparers are plausibly ~50-60% of wages (hypothesis; the record's $62,700 revenue/employee — a quarter of the CPA-office figure — confirms a low-wage, seasonal workforce)
role_hints.note: Interview-driven 1040 preparation on top of guided software is the canonical AI-automatable task; the preparer already follows a decision tree the software provides. Remaining human value is document intake trust, ITIN/EITC due diligence and refund-product handling.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.1% at 4-digit; owner comp largely SDE outside wages; hypothesis ~15-20% for this code
role_hints.note: Local marketing, seasonal hiring and multi-unit operations; in franchise systems the client file and brand often belong to the franchisor, not the local owner.

role_hints.role: Office managers / seasonal supervisors and reviewers
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-1011 First-Line Supervisors 1.88% + 13-2011 Accountants (review layer) at 4-digit; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Quality review and exception handling; AI review tools compress this layer.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-xxxx office/admin occupations in the 5412 tail (43-6014 Secretaries 2.02%, 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.44% at 4-digit); hypothesis ~15%
role_hints.note: Appointment setting, document intake and status calls are fully automatable.

source_hints.source: IRS filing-season statistics (e-file, Direct File usage) and IRS Return Preparer Office PTIN counts
source_hints.why: Official data on DIY vs. paid-preparer share and the preparer population — the substitution trend line.

source_hints.source: H&R Block and Intuit 10-Ks / investor materials
source_hints.why: Assisted vs. DIY client counts, pricing per return and AI-assisted prep disclosures — the best public window into segment economics.

source_hints.source: NATP (National Association of Tax Professionals) Tax Professional Fee Study
source_hints.why: Per-return fee benchmarks by form type for independent preparers — the pricing evidence base. Note: the National Society of Accountants' rival Income & Fees survey may have lapsed after the 2023 NSA-NATP merger; verify and use NATP data.

source_hints.source: Franchise Disclosure Documents (H&R Block, Liberty Tax, Jackson Hewitt)
source_hints.why: Unit-level revenue, royalty loads and refund-product economics for the franchise segment.

source_hints.source: Accounting Practice Sales tax-practice listings and franchise resale brokers
source_hints.why: Small tax-practice multiples (quoted on gross revenue with owner comp inside) and franchise resale values.

source_hints.source: IRS PTIN holder statistics by preparer age/credential where published; NATP membership demographics
source_hints.why: The only visible owner-demographic data for independents; expect thin coverage and say so.

capture_questions: Pricing is a flat fee per return, benchmarked against free alternatives (TurboTax free tier, IRS Direct File, VITA): when AI removes the labor from preparation, does per-return competition and free-alternative pressure force fees down toward zero for simple returns, or does the storefront hold price because its customers are buying refund advances, in-person trust and EITC due-diligence handling rather than form completion?

What share of clients are EITC/refund-advance filers who come for the money product (refund transfer/advance) rather than the prep — and does that embedded-lending relationship insulate the storefront from free AI filing?

Does IRS Direct File expansion (and state equivalents) plus generic consumer AI eliminate the paid simple-1040 segment outright, and how fast is state adoption expanding? Verify Direct File's current status — its funding and scope have been politically contested.
In franchise systems, who owns the client file — brand or franchisee — and what does royalty load plus franchisor file ownership do to the durability and margin of a local acquisition?
terminal_value_question: Simple-return preparation is arguably the most AI-terminal paid service in this set: no license beyond a PTIN protects it, the product is a government form, and free AI-grade alternatives already exist. Within 5-10 years, does storefront prep survive only as embedded refund lending plus complex/small-business/immigrant (ITIN) and non-filer-resolution niches — and what fraction of today's revenue do those represent? Distinguish AI cutting the storefront's seasonal labor cost from AI (and free government filing) removing the reason customers walk in at all.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate: flat per-return fees against free substitutes is the steepest deflation setup in the accounting group — model pi_dist with the free-alternative benchmark named, and separate refund-product revenue (fee-per-product, deflation-resistant, but regulatorily exposed) from prep-fee revenue.

Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5412 and is dominated by CPA-firm occupations; this code's real workforce is seasonal preparers and intake staff. The record's revenue/employee of $62,700 (vs. $263,807 at 541211) is the tell — use the hypothesis structure and flag it.
Extreme seasonality: most revenue lands January-April; SUSB/QCEW annual-average employment understates peak staffing and any part-year financials distort margins — normalize to full-year.

EBITDA normalization: independents are SDE businesses with owner comp inside quoted multiples; franchise units carry royalty loads (verify current % from FDDs) that independents lack — do not mix the two comp sets.

Boundary confusion: complex/business returns and CPA storefronts belong to 541211; enrolled agents' accounting practices may sit in 541219 — this code is dominated by consumer 1040 volume, so do not import CPA-firm pricing power evidence.

Size-structure reality check: n_band is only 40 firms (all in the 100-499 employee class) out of 19,464 — the acquirable universe at $1-10M EBITDA is tiny and skews to multi-unit franchise operators and regional chains; single storefronts fall far below the band.
